text: Sharing a Hartman reversal in a patient who developed a radiation-induced, very complex rectovaginal fistula that was repaired about three months earlier. This is a gastrograph in enema showing that the fistula has completely healed. This was confirmed on pre-surgical endoscopy. And now she's going to undergo a Hartman reversal.

This is a gastrograph in enema showing that the fistula has completely healed. This was confirmed on pre-surgical endoscopy. And now she's going to undergo a Hartman reversal.
The surgeon mobilizes the distal segment of the bowel, which is scarred and folded within the pelvis, carefully separating it from surrounding adhesions.
The surgeon mobilizes the distal segment of the bowel, which is scarred and folded within the pelvis, carefully undoing the folds to restore its position.

Patient had had radiation for a very complex GYN cancer. And that was the cause of the fistula that she developed. There was no evidence of recurrent cancer for several years. The fistula repair was done approximately three months earlier.

The robotic repair involves dissection below the rectovaginal septum, where the surgeon separates the fistula and closes both the vaginal and rectal defects. A tongue of omentum is placed in the area, and the previously present perineal fistula is now completely closed.

The robotic repair involves dissection below the rectovaginal septum, separating the fistula. The surgeon closes the vaginal and rectal defects and places a tongue of omentum in the area. The perineal fistula is noted to be completely closed.
The surgeon mobilizes the proximal bowel to gain length, using the colostomy site as access to the abdomen. A long silk thread is visible on the end of the colostomy for identification and traction.

The surgeon mobilizes the proximal bowel to gain additional length, using a long silk thread for identification and traction. The colostomy site serves as access to the abdomen. The tongue of omentum is visible to the right, near the previous fistula site. The surgeon unfolds the sigmoid loop, achieving more length and eliminating tension.

The surgeon observes the silk thread used for identification and traction, noting the adequate length of the bowel. The tongue of omentum is visible to the right, positioned near the previous fistula site. The surgeon unfolds the sigmoid loop, increasing bowel length and eliminating tension.

The surgeon lines up a hand-sewn anastomosis, using a stay suture of three silk on the far corner. Arm number one holds the suture in place while the surgeon sews the two staple lines together using a 3-0 nine-inch V-lock suture, completing the anastomosis between the two ends of the bowel.

The surgeon lines up a hand-sewn anastomosis using a stay suture on the far corner with 3-0 silk. The suture is held up with the robotic arm, and the two staple lines of the bowel ends are sewn together using a 3-0 V-lock suture.

In this case I think it worked out really nicely as well because it would have been difficult even with an extra long EEA to pass it up that high. Alternatively you could easily have done a side to side with a GIA and then a hand sewn closure of the common channel. But this whole case only took about an hour and a half so I thought this was pretty nice.

I did do this case with a diverting loop ileostomy due to the prior radiation, just not taking any chances. The patient was aware that I was going to be doing this pre-surgery, so there was no confusion there.
The surgeon performs a colotomy, inspecting for fusion during the process. The colotomy is visible, followed by the proctotomy, with the proximal and distal holes clearly defined.

The surgeon identifies the collotomy and proctotomy sites, noting the presence of sigmoid tissue from the initial Hartmann procedure. The proximal and distal holes are visible, and the surgeon begins sewing with a 3-0 V-Lock suture, starting at the center and using a baseball stitch technique to secure the tissue.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Lock suture to perform a baseball stitch, starting in the center and sewing outward around the far corner. Each suture is precisely placed and cinched down. The stitching continues from the middle towards the surgeon, around the near corner, and then over the top.

The surgeon places each suture precisely at the far corner, then begins sewing from the middle towards themselves, cinching down each suture. The suturing continues around the near corner, followed by canalization over the top of the anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the anterior part of the enterotomy's common channel using the original inner stitch, followed by sewing the posterior outer row anteriorly across the top of the anastomosis.

The surgeon closes the anterior part of the enterotomy's common channel using the original inner stitch, demonstrating a technique for sewn anastomosis. The posterior outer row is then sewn anteriorly across the top, completing the secure anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the procedure and performs a rigid scope examination to check the distal fistula site, followed by a leak test in the rectum and vagina, both of which are negative. A flexible endoscopy is conducted past the anastomosis, confirming it is widely patent and well perfused. The procedure concludes with the patient being diverted with a loop ileostomy.

And you've seen in a bunch of my posts, this is pretty much my go-to, unless I do an end-to-end with a transrectal extraction and void the linear staplers altogether. After we finish this off, we're going to go ahead and do a rigid scope and a checking of the distal fistula site and a leak test as well, both in the rectum and the vagina, which was negative. I did not necessarily do a leak test of the anastomosis given its location, but I did do a flexible endoscopy up past it. It was widely patent, well perfused. And then again, the patient was diverted with a loop ileostomy at the end of the case.
